address_hint: Al Waab St, next to Aspire Tower
blurb_en: Qatar’s largest urban park, with playgrounds, a lake, skate ramps, and long shaded paths. Good at sunrise or after 4pm. The loop is 2.2 km — strollers handle it easily.
honest_note: Midday in summer is brutal; aim for early morning or after sunset. Weekends get packed near the playground.

blurb_en: Sprawling park across from the Corniche with several themed play zones, a skate park, outdoor gyms and an amphitheatre. Lots of shade at the north end. Food trucks on Friday evenings.
honest_note: Weekday mornings are calm. Fridays get very busy near the splash area.

blurb_en: A waterfront park with skyline views, gentle slopes for scooters, and a playground at the eastern end. Pair it with the museum cafe for a short outing with under-8s.
honest_note: Parking fills on weekends; use the museum lot or walk in from the Corniche.

blurb_en: Designed as a running and walking park with long, shaded undulating paths. The playground near the east gate works well for ages 3–9. Breezy even in hotter months.
honest_note: Play equipment can get hot mid-afternoon; pick morning or evening.

blurb_en: Calm, supervised beach with shallow entry and watersports rentals at the Pearl end. Sunshades available to rent. Pair it with a walk through Katara after sunset.
honest_note: Afternoons bake in summer — mornings are safer for under-5s.

blurb_en: Long, shallow beach with shaded gazebos, BBQ pits, and a playground at the north end. A solid Saturday-morning pick if Corniche feels too busy.
honest_note: Sand gets hot; bring water shoes for toddlers.

address_hint: Between Lusail and Al Khor, coastal road
blurb_en: Quiet stretch of coast north of Lusail, shallow for a long way out. Popular with families for kite flying in the cooler months.
honest_note: No café or shade — bring everything you need.

blurb_en: Not a kids venue per se, but the falcon souq, horse stables and toy-shop alley make a great after-sunset walk for ages 4+. Try one of the courtyard restaurants when the band is playing.
honest_note: Strollers struggle on the cobblestones. Evenings get very crowded on weekends.

address_hint: West Bay coastal, north of Pearl
blurb_en: Open-air village with amphitheatre, galleries, a kids’ reading nook at Katara Library, and seasonal festivals for ages 5+. Good for a two-hour evening loop.
honest_note: Very few covered walkways in summer — plan for sunset.

blurb_en: Flat, stroller-friendly promenade around the Porto Arabia marina. Works as a pre-dinner walk with scooters for ages 4–9.
honest_note: Parking in the basement garages is paid after the first hour on weekends.

blurb_en: A long, tiled promenade with fountains that run at sunset and plenty of open space for scooters and bikes. Best in winter months.
honest_note: Exposed and windy — not great for toddlers with a dummy.

address_hint: West Bay to MIA, 7 km walk
blurb_en: The classic 7 km Doha waterfront. Cycle lanes on the promenade, bike hire at several points, dhow trips from the middle section. Works for every age from strollers up.
honest_note: Parking is painful on National Day and NYE fireworks evenings.

blurb_en: A deep natural sinkhole turned family park with a big playground, trails, and a small pond. Quieter than Aspire.
honest_note: Fewer cafes nearby — bring snacks.

blurb_en: A long linear park through Gharrafa with play zones every few hundred metres. Good for families with kids of different ages — one play area doesn’t hold everyone.
honest_note: Limited parking at busy times; street-park on the side roads.

blurb_en: Big, green park with a small train, dolphin fountain, and a sprawling playground. Worth the drive if you live north of Lusail.
honest_note: Facilities are older; the toilets show their age.

blurb_en: The indoor garden in the transit terminal — mostly a layover pick, but worth a mention if you’re on a long transit with kids.
honest_note: Transit only; not a day-out destination.

blurb_en: Qatar’s landmark art museum. Family trails and the weekend kids’ workshop program make it work for ages 5+. The top-floor café has the best city view in Doha.
honest_note: Some galleries are quiet — bring headphones or a hushed 5-year-old.

name_en: Mathaf: Arab Museum of Modern Art
blurb_en: Modern Arab art in a converted school building, with a family Fridays programme aimed at ages 5–12. Smaller than MIA, easier on short attention spans.
honest_note: Shows rotate — check what’s up before you drive out.

blurb_en: A 1.5 km immersive walk through Qatar’s natural and social history, designed for all ages. The children’s museum wing is a highlight for ages 5–10.
honest_note: Full loop is long — strollers make it manageable, but under-3s will fade.

blurb_en: Four restored heritage houses covering slavery, family life, oil, and architecture in Qatar. Best for ages 7+ with a guide or family trail booklet.
honest_note: Heavier topics in parts — preview the slavery exhibit before taking under-8s.

blurb_en: Interactive sports museum with hands-on stations (reaction tests, a climbing wall, a Paralympic simulator). Good for ages 5–14.
honest_note: Some interactive stations queue on weekends.

blurb_en: A dazzling Koolhaas-designed library with one of the best kids’ sections in the Gulf. Storytime weekly, plus school-holiday workshops. Free membership for residents.
honest_note: Exam season (May, December) fills the quiet zones with teenagers.

blurb_en: A small, quiet library with an Arabic-first children’s corner. Drop-in on weekday mornings with a 2–5 year old and it feels like your own.
honest_note: Limited seating on Friday mornings during storytime.

blurb_en: Screen-free workshops for kids that lean into craft, presence and connection rather than play-for-play’s sake. Not a daycare, not a playground — worth a look for ages 3–12.
honest_note: New opening; book via Instagram to confirm.

blurb_en: A hands-on children’s play museum, with themed galleries for ages 1–12. If confirmed in Qatar, it would sit near the top of our weekend picks.
honest_note: Confirming Qatar operating status before publish.

blurb_en: Indoor miniature city where kids play grown-up jobs — fire fighter, dentist, baker, pilot. Best experienced as a 4-hour block with ages 4–12.
honest_note: The parent area has limited seating; be ready to watch rather than sit.

blurb_en: Indoor theme park with rides graded from toddler to teen. Weekday afternoons have short queues; weekends are a commitment.
honest_note: Height restrictions cut out some rides for under-110 cm — check before paying the bundle.

blurb_en: The ice rink, mini bowling, and indoor coaster inside Villaggio. Works well as a 2-hour stop combined with lunch.
honest_note: Weekends get very loud in the coaster area.

blurb_en: Trampoline park with dodgeball, a foam pit and a dedicated toddler zone. Grip socks sold at entry.
honest_note: Loud and energetic — sensory-sensitive kids should visit at opening time.

blurb_en: Trampoline arena with basketball dunk lanes and a Ninja Warrior course. Best for confident jumpers ages 7+.
honest_note: Toddlers are better served at Flip Out’s toddler zone.

blurb_en: Big indoor adventure park with a ropes course, slides and a climbing tower. Ages 3+ with a separate toddler area.
honest_note: Socks required; QAR 15 if you forget them.

blurb_en: Qatar’s largest indoor theme park, with record-holding coasters. For thrill-seeker teens, this is the main event.
honest_note: Ticketing can be confusing — understand the pass tiers before arriving.

blurb_en: Edutainment space where kids play real-world professions in miniature — newsroom, airport, clinic. Overlaps with KidzMondo; pick based on neighbourhood.
honest_note: Book a time slot — walk-ins wait at busy times.

blurb_en: The Venice-themed mall with Gondolania inside. A reliable rainy-day combo of food court, ice rink and indoor coaster.
honest_note: Weekend afternoons are the peak for everything — aim for 10–11 am.

blurb_en: Hosts KidzMondo, the Oasis cinema and a large food court. Good option when you need air-conditioned hours with mixed ages.
honest_note: Parking gets tight on weekend evenings.

blurb_en: Mall with Angry Birds World, Juniverse and Virtuocity under one roof. A solid rainy-day pick if you have mixed ages.
honest_note: The coaster inside Angry Birds World is noisy; not great for sensory-sensitive kids.

blurb_en: Luxury mall with a large fountain show, outdoor canal and several family restaurants. The fountain show at sunset holds under-6s for 10 minutes.
honest_note: Limited kids-specific play — treat it as a sunset walk.

blurb_en: Older mall with a renovated family floor. Quieter than the big four; a good mid-week pick.
honest_note: Cinema schedule thin on weekdays.

blurb_en: City Center still has one of the better ice rinks and a solid Fun City arcade. Easy access from West Bay offices.
honest_note: Layout dated — plan routes with a stroller.

blurb_en: Small, quiet mall with a kids’ salon and a café upstairs that’s friendly to buggies.
honest_note: Not a destination — pair with a walk along the Corniche.

blurb_en: Community mall with a large indoor play centre and a trampoline zone. Strong local following in the north.
honest_note: Tight car park at school run times.
